  1. 🐼 Data Manipulation
  2. Data Manipulation with Pandas

Data Manipulation with Pandas

Merge & Join, GroupBy, Pivot Tables, and String Operations

Author

S.A.M.Perera - AS2022296

Introduction

This chapter introduces advanced data manipulation techniques in Pandas. These techniques allow us to combine datasets, summarize grouped data, create multidimensional summaries, and efficiently process textual information.

The main topics covered are:

  • Merge & Join
  • Aggregation & GroupBy
  • Pivot Tables
  • Vectorized String Operations
Code 
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

03 Combining Datasets: Merge & Join

Introduction

Data are often stored across multiple tables. To perform meaningful analyses, we frequently need to combine these tables based on common variables. Pandas provides powerful relational operations through merge() and join().

The merge operation is conceptually similar to SQL joins.

Three Categories of Joins

One-to-One Joins

A one-to-one join occurs when each key value appears exactly once in both tables.

Code 
df1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee": ["Bob", "Jake", "Lisa", "Sue"],
    "group": ["Accounting", "Engineering", "Engineering", "HR"]
})

df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee": ["Lisa", "Bob", "Jake", "Sue"],
    "hire_date": [2004, 2008, 2012, 2014]
})

pd.merge(df1, df2)

Many-to-One Joins

A many-to-one join occurs when multiple observations in one table correspond to a single observation in another table.

Code 
df3 = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": ["Accounting", "Engineering", "HR"],
    "supervisor": ["Carly", "Guido", "Steve"]
})

pd.merge(df1, df3)

Many-to-Many Joins

A many-to-many join occurs when duplicate keys exist in both tables.

Code 
df4 = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": ["Accounting", "Accounting",
              "Engineering", "Engineering",
              "HR", "HR"],
    "skills": ["math", "spreadsheets",
               "coding", "linux",
               "communication", "recruiting"]
})

pd.merge(df1, df4)

Specifying the Merge Key

Using the Same Column Name

When both DataFrames contain a common column name, the key can be specified using the on parameter.

Code 
pd.merge(df1, df2, on="employee")

Using Different Column Names

Code 
df5 = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["Bob", "Jake", "Lisa", "Sue"],
    "salary": [50000, 70000, 80000, 60000]
})

pd.merge(
    df1,
    df5,
    left_on="employee",
    right_on="name"
)

Merging on Index

Code 
df1a = df1.set_index("employee")
df2a = df2.set_index("employee")

pd.merge(
    df1a,
    df2a,
    left_index=True,
    right_index=True
)

Using .join()

The .join() method is a convenient shortcut for index-based merges.

Code 
df1a.join(df2a)

Set Arithmetic and the how Parameter

The how parameter controls which keys appear in the merged result.

Inner Join

Keeps only keys that appear in both tables.

Code 
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="inner")

Outer Join

Keeps all keys from both tables.

Code 
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="outer")

Left Join

Keeps all keys from the left table.

Code 
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="left")

Right Join

Keeps all keys from the right table.

Code 
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="right")

04 Aggregation & GroupBy

Introduction

GroupBy is one of the most important tools in data analysis. It allows us to divide observations into groups and calculate summary statistics for each group.

The GroupBy operation follows the Split–Apply–Combine paradigm.

  1. Split the data into groups.
  2. Apply a function to each group.
  3. Combine the results.

Sample Dataset

Code 
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "key": ["A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C"],
    "data1": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],
    "data2": [7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12]
})

df

Split–Apply–Combine

Code 
df.groupby("key").sum()

Aggregation Functions

Aggregation reduces each group to one or more summary values.

Mean

Code 
df.groupby("key").mean()

Median

Code 
df.groupby("key").median()

Multiple Aggregations

Code 
df.groupby("key").aggregate(
    ["min", np.median, max]
)

Filtering Groups

Filtering removes groups that fail a specified condition.

Code 
def filter_func(x):
    return x["data2"].std() > 1

df.groupby("key").filter(filter_func)

Transform

Transform returns a result having the same shape as the original dataset.

Code 
df.groupby("key").transform(
    lambda x: x - x.mean()
)

Apply

Apply is the most flexible GroupBy operation.

Code 
def normalize(group):
    group["data1"] = group["data1"] / group["data1"].sum()
    return group

df.groupby("key").apply(normalize)

Common Aggregation Methods

Method Description
count() Count non-missing values
sum() Sum of values
mean() Arithmetic mean
median() Median
min() Minimum value
max() Maximum value
std() Standard deviation
var() Variance
first() First observation
last() Last observation
describe() Summary statistics

05 Pivot Tables

Introduction

Pivot tables provide an efficient way to summarize multidimensional data.

A pivot table combines grouping, aggregation, and reshaping into a single operation.

Sample Dataset

Code 
titanic = pd.DataFrame({
    "sex": ["male","female","female","male","female","male"],
    "class": ["First","First","Third","Third","Second","Second"],
    "survived": [0,1,1,0,1,0]
})

titanic

GroupBy Approach

The following analysis computes survival rates by sex and passenger class.

Code 
titanic.groupby(
    ["sex", "class"]
)["survived"].mean().unstack()

Pivot Table Approach

The same result can be obtained more concisely using a pivot table.

Code 
titanic.pivot_table(
    values="survived",
    index="sex",
    columns="class"
)

Margins

The margins=True argument adds overall row and column totals.

Code 
titanic.pivot_table(
    values="survived",
    index="sex",
    columns="class",
    margins=True
)

Fill Missing Values

Missing cells can be replaced using fill_value.

Code 
titanic.pivot_table(
    values="survived",
    index="sex",
    columns="class",
    fill_value=0
)

Multiple Grouping Variables

Code 
titanic["age_group"] = [
    "Adult",
    "Adult",
    "Child",
    "Adult",
    "Child",
    "Adult"
]

titanic.pivot_table(
    values="survived",
    index=["sex", "age_group"],
    columns="class"
)

06 Vectorized String Operations

Introduction

Text data frequently appear in real-world datasets. Pandas provides the .str accessor to perform vectorized string operations efficiently.

Advantages include:

  • Handles missing values automatically.
  • Faster than explicit loops.
  • Supports regular expressions.
  • Produces cleaner and more readable code.

Sample Data

Code 
data = ["peter", "Paul", None, "MARY"]

names = pd.Series(data)

names

Basic String Methods

Capitalize

Code 
names.str.capitalize()

Lowercase

Code 
names.str.lower()

Uppercase

Code 
names.str.upper()

Title Case

Code 
names.str.title()

Length

Code 
names.str.len()

Remove Whitespace

Code 
pd.Series([" hello ", " world "]).str.strip()

Regular Expressions

Contains

Search for a pattern.

Code 
names.str.contains(
    "a",
    case=False,
    na=False
)

Replace

Replace matching patterns.

Code 
names.str.replace(
    "a",
    "*",
    regex=True
)

Extract

Extract portions of text using regular expressions.

Code 
emails = pd.Series([
    "alice@gmail.com",
    "bob@yahoo.com"
])

emails.str.extract(
    r"(.*)@(.*)"
)

Splitting Strings

Code 
pd.Series([
    "A-B-C",
    "D-E-F"
]).str.split("-")

One-Hot Encoding with get_dummies()

Code 
pd.Series([
    "A|B",
    "B|C",
    "A|C"
]).str.get_dummies("|")

Recipe Database Example

Suppose we have recipe data containing ingredient lists.

Code 
recipes = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["Recipe1", "Recipe2", "Recipe3"],
    "ingredients": [
        "parsley paprika tarragon",
        "salt pepper oregano",
        "parsley paprika"
    ]
})

recipes

Creating Ingredient Indicators

Code 
spice_list = [
    "parsley",
    "paprika",
    "tarragon",
    "salt",
    "pepper"
]

spice_df = pd.DataFrame({
    spice: recipes["ingredients"].str.contains(
        spice,
        case=False,
        na=False
    )
    for spice in spice_list
})

spice_df

Finding Recipes with Specific Ingredients

Code 
mask = (
    spice_df["parsley"] &
    spice_df["paprika"] &
    spice_df["tarragon"]
)

recipes.loc[mask, ["name"]]

Key Takeaways

Merge & Join

  • Combines datasets using common keys.
  • Supports one-to-one, many-to-one, and many-to-many joins.
  • how= controls inner, outer, left, and right joins.

GroupBy

  • Implements the Split–Apply–Combine paradigm.
  • Supports aggregation, filtering, transformation, and custom operations.

Pivot Tables

  • Provide concise multidimensional summaries.
  • Combine grouping and aggregation in a single operation.
  • Support margins and missing-value handling.

String Operations

  • .str enables vectorized text processing.
  • Supports built-in string methods and regular expressions.
  • Handles missing values automatically.
  • get_dummies() converts categorical text into indicator variables.
